text: From Newcastle Hospital, this is Ahmed Khabib. I'd like to present to you this fifty-year-old lady that had progressive chest infections and over the last twelve months started to complain of neutropenic sepsis with frequent admissions into the intensive care unit. She was worked up and found to have thymoma in the anterior mediastinum.

The surgeon uses three ports on the left side, including a submemory incision and axillary access, to approach the thymic lobe. The mediastinal pleura is opened anterior to the left phrenic nerve, and the lump is lifted inferiorly, releasing it from the fibrous pericardium. Dissection continues beneath the mass, lifting it off the pulmonary trunk and the ascending and arch of the aorta. The surgeon carefully dissects around the blood vessels draining into and out of the left internal mammary artery and vein, avoiding injury to the left phrenic nerve. The tumor appears aggressive and invasive. Retraction is applied to the left biomic horn, following it up to the thyroid.

The surgeon uses a hook and bipolar fenestrated forceps to support and drag down the thymic tissue, revealing vessels at the top of the thymocorne originating from the thyroid. The vessels are clipped for safety before using diathermy to divide them. The procedure progresses towards the right side, releasing tissue around the right thymic horn. The dissection approaches the confluence of the innominate veins into the superior vena cava (SVC), where Hemolock clips are applied to isolate the vein draining into the SVC. Careful blunt dissection is performed to avoid injuring the right phrenic nerve, which runs from the right internal mammary base onto the lateral side of the SVC.

The surgeon identifies a lymph node and uses a bipolar device to safely divide tissue without conducting electricity to the phrenic nerve. The right pleura is opened to enhance visualization. An endocatch bag is inserted through a 12mm axillary port to retrieve the specimen, ensuring no sealing occurs in the left pleura. The innominate vein and aorta are visibly clear of tissue post-resection, with a comparison image shown before and after tumor removal.
Thank you for watching. This is Ahmed Habib from New Cross Hospital.
